structures.name: PFA
structures.legalNature: Individual business form under OUG 44/2008. No separate legal personality.
structures.liabilityDescription: Personal liability — business patrimony first, then personal assets.
structures.taxLogic: Personal income tax (10%) on net income + CAS (25%) and CASS (10%) based on threshold bands.
pros: Simplest setup and lowest admin overhead
Single-entry bookkeeping — no accountant required for basic cases
Freedom to contract with multiple clients
Can hire up to 3 employees if needed
cons: CAS/CASS contributions can be high on high-margin income
No ability to retain profits in entity for reinvestment
CASS scales with income up to 72× min wage cap (2026)
structures.bestFor: Solo IT contractors with straightforward service income, low overhead, and no need for corporate structure.
structures.unsuitableWhen: You need more than 5 CAEN classes, more than 3 employees, or want limited liability and profit retention.

structures.name: II
structures.legalNature: Individual enterprise form under OUG 44/2008. No separate legal personality.
structures.liabilityDescription: Personal liability — same model as PFA.
structures.taxLogic: Same as PFA — personal income tax (10%) on net income + CAS/CASS.
pros: More scaling room than PFA — 10 CAEN classes, 8 employees
Same simple tax logic as PFA
cons: Same CAS/CASS burden as PFA at high margins
No profit retention or corporate finance flexibility
Still limited in employees vs SRL
structures.bestFor: IT contractors who need more CAEN classes or employees than PFA allows, but still want simplicity.
structures.unsuitableWhen: You need more than 10 CAEN classes, more than 8 employees, or want limited liability.

structures.name: IF
structures.legalNature: Family enterprise under OUG 44/2008. No separate legal personality. Requires 2+ family members.
structures.liabilityDescription: Members jointly and severally liable per participation quotas.
structures.taxLogic: Same as PFA but income split among members by agreement. Each member computes own CAS/CASS/PIT.
pros: Income splitting among family members can reduce per-member CAS/CASS burden
Can be useful for family-run IT businesses
structures.bestFor: Family-run IT businesses where income splitting among members provides tax advantage.
structures.unsuitableWhen: You are operating solo, need employees, or want limited liability. Not practical for typical solo IT contracting.
structures.forcedChangeTriggersDescription: Want non-family employees or operating solo.

structures.name: SRL
structures.legalNature: Limited liability company with separate legal personality under company law.
structures.liabilityDescription: Limited liability — shareholders liable only up to subscribed share capital (with anti-abuse exceptions).
structures.taxLogic: Company tax (micro 1% on revenue or 16% CIT on profit) + payout tax (salary deductions + 16% dividend withholding + possible CASS on dividends).
pros: Micro regime: only 1% tax on revenue (if eligible)
Flexibility in payout timing and strategy (salary vs dividends vs retain)
Can reinvest profits inside the company
Best for mixing services + product revenue streams
No cap on employees or CAEN classes
cons: Higher fixed admin costs (accountant, filings, payroll)
Micro ceiling at €100k — regime loss risk if exceeded
Must have at least 1 employee for micro regime
structures.bestFor: IT contractors with high revenue who want tax optimization via salary/dividend mix, limited liability, and profit retention.
structures.unsuitableWhen: Very low revenue that doesn't justify admin costs, or you strongly prefer simplicity over tax optimization.
structures.forcedChangeTriggersDescription: Micro: revenue > €100k → switches to 16% CIT from that quarter. Employee condition must be met within 90 days for new company.
